content.subtitle: What is a conditional sentence?
items: A conditional sentence contains the word **if** and describes a situation along with its possible result.
Every conditional has two parts: the **if-clause** (the condition) and the **main clause** (the result).
The type of conditional we choose depends on whether the situation is real, possible or imaginary.

content.subtitle: Useful notes
items: When the **if-clause** comes first, we put a comma before the result clause.
✓ **If it snows tomorrow, we'll cancel the trip.**
When the result clause comes first, a comma is not usually needed.
✓ **We'll cancel the trip if it snows tomorrow.**

content.subtitle: Use
rows: To talk about general truths, habits or scientific facts — things that are always or usually true
If you **leave** metal in the rain, it **rusts**.

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: if + present simple, will + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
rows: To talk about real or likely events in the present or future and their probable results
If you **don't leave** now, you **'ll miss** the train.

content.subtitle: Useful notes
content.description: Instead of **will**, we can use other modals to add different shades of meaning.
items: **If you rest today, you might feel better by tomorrow.** (possibility)
We can also use an imperative to give direct advice or instructions: **If you're cold, put on a jacket!**

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: if + past simple, would + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
rows: To talk about situations that are imaginary, impossible or very unlikely in the present or future
If I **lived** closer to work, I **would cycle** every day.

content.subtitle: Useful notes
content.description: The second conditional is also used to give advice, usually with **If I were you ...**
items: **If I were you, I'd speak to the manager directly.** (more formal)
**If I was you, I'd speak to the manager directly.** (more informal)

sections.title: E: Original course practice
sections.instructions: Complete each original item using the target language from this lesson.

items.sentence: During the class project on Conditionals (zero, first & second), Mia wrote ...... to complete the model sentence.
items.answer: If I had more money, I could buy a car.

items.sentence: In an original course practice item about Conditionals (zero, first & second), the best completion is ...... .
items.answer: If she studies harder, she will pass the exam.

items.sentence: Sara checked the course practice notes and added ...... to the example.
items.answer: Water boils when the temperature reaches 100°C.

items.sentence: The online lesson included a short sentence about Conditionals (zero, first & second): the missing part is ...... .
items.answer: If you take this medicine, you will feel better.

items.sentence: During the school club on Conditionals (zero, first & second), Nina wrote ...... to complete the model sentence.
items.answer: If I were you, I wouldn't complain so much.

items.sentence: In an original course practice item about Conditionals (zero, first & second), the best completion is ...... .
items.answer: Unless she practises every day, she won't improve.
